title: Top Five Movies Time Forgot. Plus: Why the Beatles Movie Is No Longer a Movie.
format: Peter Jackson's Beatles documentary gets demoted from a theatrical release to a Disney Plus miniseries, which turns into a conversation about movies refusing to end. Then Alex Ross Perry joins to dig up the films that vanished from availability entirely.

segments.heading: Get Back Becomes a TV Show
segments.note: Sean and Amanda on movies that won't end

films.title: No Sudden Move
films.note: Sean's counterexample to the bloat: Soderbergh's new one runs an hour fifty, closes every loop in the plot, and gets in and out.

films.title: Cruella
films.note: Cited as a recent example of a movie stretched to two hours and twenty minutes for no reason anyone can defend.

films.title: The Tomorrow War
films.note: Same complaint: no reason a movie like this needs to run two hours and twenty minutes.

films.title: Iron Man
films.note: Sean pinpoints this as the moment he should've clocked that studios were done telling standalone stories and started building worlds instead.

segments.heading: The Movies Time Forgot
segments.note: hard-to-find classics and misunderstood flops, oldest to newest

films.title: Wind Across the Everglades
films.note: Nicholas Ray's Florida conservationist drama, written by Budd Schulberg and starring Christopher Plummer. Alex caught it at a Film Forum Ray series around 2006 and it hasn't screened since; never got a tape release, DVD, Blu-ray, or streaming spot.

films.title: Health
films.note: Robert Altman's satire of self-care culture, remembered mostly for Ronald Reagan calling it the world's worst movie. No disc or streaming release; only surfaces at retrospectives.

films.title: Brewster McCloud
films.note: Another Altman that vanished, this one for years despite being a lot of people's favorite of his; only became available again a few years before this taping.

films.title: Donovan's Reef
films.note: John Ford and John Wayne, with Lee Marvin as his drinking-and-fighting foil. The one non-canonical entry in an otherwise undisputed run of Ford/Wayne collaborations, and currently inaccessible.

films.title: Patty Hearst
films.note: Paul Schrader's docudrama, one of the two Schrader films Alex hadn't seen before finally watching a pristine print at the Harvard Film Archive. Briefly on the Criterion Channel, then pulled; the Twilight Time Blu-ray is now out of print since that label folded.

films.title: The Touch
films.note: Ingmar Bergman's English-language film with Elliott Gould, who reportedly still owns the rights. Alex calls it a four-star Bergman on par with The Virgin Spring and Cries and Whispers, buried by the assumption you'd need to work through 40 other Bergmans first.

films.title: M Butterfly
films.note: David Cronenberg's adaptation of the play, with Jeremy Irons, John Lone, and Ian Richardson. Alex, a Cronenberg completist, says it was only ever on tape and is virtually impossible to see now.

films.title: Fast Company
films.note: Cronenberg's drag-racing movie, the one Alex held off on for years because renting the tape meant taking home two other movies instead. He finally saw it at a festival in Slovenia in 2011.

films.title: One from the Heart
films.note: Coppola's impressionistic musical, the movie that nearly bankrupted him. Both hosts consider it the best thing he's made, buried under a bomb reputation it never fully earned.

films.title: Daisy Miller
films.note: Peter Bogdanovich and Cybill Shepherd's Henry James adaptation. Alex has watched it four or five times and still thinks it's where Bogdanovich went south.

films.title: Irreconcilable Differences
films.note: Nancy Meyers's directing-couple satire, with Sharon Stone doing a deliberately terrible Cybill Shepherd impression. Watching it gave Sean more sympathy for Shepherd's Daisy Miller performance.

films.title: The Ghost Writer
films.note: Polanski's Nantucket-set thriller with Ewan McGregor, Pierce Brosnan, and Olivia Williams. Sean tells Alex he once told McGregor to his face that it's a masterpiece, and McGregor said no one had ever said that to him before.

films.title: Bye Bye Braverman
films.note: Sidney Lumet's comedy with George Segal and Jessica Walter, made around the same time as The Group and The Pawnbroker. Sean says he's never met anyone who's seen it.

segments.heading: Alex's Quick List
segments.note: five more, rattled off at the end

picks.title: The Road to Glory
picks.note: One of Alex's favorite Howard Hawks films, with, he says, a devastating final shot. Currently has no availability whatsoever.

picks.title: Whity
picks.note: One leg of a Fassbinder triptych Alex ranks below the other two.

picks.title: Jailbait
picks.note: Fassbinder's made-for-TV film about a teenage girl and the older man she's involved with; caused a stir when it screened at MoMA and hasn't been restored since.

picks.title: Lili Marleen
picks.note: A big-budget Fassbinder war film Alex compares to Verhoeven's Black Book.

picks.title: King Lear
picks.note: Godard's version, with Molly Ringwald and a brief Woody Allen appearance. Every other Cannon Films release is available; this one isn't.
